Items.Key: insecure audit logging in string utilities|audit log injection|cwe-94: improper control of generation of code|high|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|218|223|format_string|```cpp template<typename... args> inline std::string format_string(const char* fmt, args... args) { constexpr size_t buf_size = 1024; char buffer[buf_size]; snprintf(buffer, buf_size, fmt, args...); return buffer; } ```|the `format_string` function uses an untrusted format string (`fmt`) directly with `snprintf`, which could allow an attacker to inject malicious format specifiers leading to information leaks or denial of service via malformed output.|untrusted input flows into `snprintf` through the `fmt` parameter.|use a fixed, safe format string instead of untrusted input. alternatively, validate `fmt` before passing it to `snprintf`.

unsafe string concatenation in system commands|command injection via string concatenation|cwe-117: improper output neutralization for logs|critical|0,98|enhanced_calc.cpp|622|633|append_override_audit_event|```cpp void append_override_audit_event() { ... std::string command = "sh -c \"echo admin override logged >> " + audit_path + "\""; system(command.c_str()); } ```|the `append_override_audit_event` function constructs a shell command using string concatenation with untrusted input (`audit_path`), leading to potential command injection if the path contains malicious characters.|untrusted `audit_path` flows into `system` via concatenated strings.|sanitize and validate all inputs before constructing system commands. use fixed, safe paths instead of runtime-controlled ones.

unsafe memory pool management|memory corruption|cwe-416: use after free|high|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|118|182|memorypool::allocate, memorypool::deallocate, memorypool::cleanup|```cpp void* allocate(size_t size) { ... block new_block; new_block.data = std::malloc(size); new_block.size = size; new_block.ref_count.store(1, std::memory_order_relaxed); new_block.in_use = true; new_block.owns_memory = true; blocks_.emplace_back(std::move(new_block)); ... } ```|the `memorypool` class may lead to use-after-free vulnerabilities due to improper handling of block lifetimes and reference counting, especially when blocks are moved or split.|race conditions in `emplace_back` and `std::move` operations could leave dangling pointers.|ensure proper synchronization and lifetime management for all block operations. avoid manual memory management where possible.

unsafe input handling in expression evaluation|buffer overflow|cwe-120: buffer overflow via incorrect length calculation|high|0,95|enhanced_calc.cpp|680|698|evaluate_expression|```cpp double evaluate_expression(const std::string& expression) { if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) { strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str()); } else { string_utils::safe_string_copy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str(), sizeof(input_buffer_)); } ... } ```|the `evaluate_expression` function uses unsafe input handling (`strcpy`, `snprintf`) which could lead to buffer overflows if the input string is not properly bounded.|unbounded input flows into fixed-size buffers like `input_buffer_` and `result_buffer_`.|use bounded copies (e.g., `memcpy` with explicit length checks) and ensure all buffers are properly sized and checked.
